SUMMARY:
Scientific consensus on climate change - Wikipedia In 2005, another joint statement from the science academies of major countries (Brazil, Canada, China, France, Germany, India, Italy, Japan, Russia, United Kingdom and the United States referred to the conclusions of the IPCC as "the international scientific consensus", and urged prompt action on both climate change mitigation and climate change adaptation. A 2016 reanalysis confirmed that "the finding of 97% consensus [that humans are causing recent global warming] in published climate research is robust and consistent with other surveys of climate scientists and peer-reviewed studies." A 2019 study found scientific consensus to be at 100%, and a 2021 study found that consensus exceeded 99%. **^**Powell, James Lawrence (15 November 2012), "The State of Climate Science: A Thorough Review of the Scientific Literature on Global Warming", _Science Progress_, retrieved 21 September 2016

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Evaluation of climate change by the scientific community

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/20200324_Global_average_temperature_-_NASA-GISS_HadCrut_NOAA_Japan_BerkeleyE.svg/500px-20200324_Global_average_temperature_-_NASA-GISS_HadCrut_NOAA_Japan_BerkeleyE.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:20200324_Global_average_temperature_-_NASA-GISS_HadCrut_NOAA_Japan_BerkeleyE.svg)

_Observed global warming:_[Global average temperature data](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Instrumental_temperature_record "Instrumental temperature record") from various scientific organizations show substantial agreement concerning the progress and extent of global warming: 1880– pairwise [correlations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Correlation "Correlation") for the four longer-term datasets are at least [99.29%](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:20200324_Global_average_temperature_-_NASA-GISS_HadCrut_NOAA_Japan_BerkeleyE.svg#Pairwise_correlation "File:20200324 Global average temperature - NASA-GISS HadCrut NOAA Japan BerkeleyE.svg").

There is a nearly unanimous [scientific consensus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus "Scientific consensus") that the [Earth has been consistently warming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") since the start of the [Industrial Revolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), that the rate of recent warming is largely unprecedented,[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-IPCC_AR6_WGI_SPM-1): 8 [[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-NCAR5_Ch2-2): 11  and that this warming is mainly the result of a rapid increase in atmospheric [carbon dioxide (CO 2)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") caused by human activities. The human activities causing this warming include [fossil fuel combustion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel"), [cement production](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cement#CO2_emissions "Cement"), and [land use](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Land_use "Land use") changes such as [deforestation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deforestation#Atmospheric "Deforestation"),[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-3): 10–11  with a significant supporting role from the other greenhouse gases such as [methane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methane "Methane") and [nitrous oxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nitrous_oxide "Nitrous oxide").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-IPCC_AR6_WGI_SPM-1): 7  This human role in climate change is considered "unequivocal" and "incontrovertible".[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-IPCC_AR6_WGI_SPM-1): 4 [[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-NCAR5_Ch2-2): 4

Nearly all actively publishing [climate scientists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climatology "Climatology") say humans are causing climate change.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-Myers_2021-4)[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-5) Surveys of the scientific literature are another way to measure scientific consensus. A 2019 review of scientific papers found the consensus on the cause of climate change to be at 100%,[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-Powell2019-6) and a 2021 study concluded that over 99% of scientific papers agree on the human cause of climate change.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-EnvRschLtrs_20211019-7) The small percentage of papers that disagreed with the consensus often contained errors or could not be replicated.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-8)

The evidence for global warming due to human influence has been recognized by the national science academies of all the major industrialized countries.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-9) In the [scientific literature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_literature "Scientific literature"), there is a very strong consensus that global surface temperatures have increased in recent decades and that the trend is caused by human-induced emissions of [greenhouse gases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas").[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-10) No scientific body of national or international standing disagrees with this view.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-11) A few organizations with members in [extractive industries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mining "Mining") hold [non-committal positions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#Non-committal_statements),[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-12) and some have tried to persuade the public that climate change is not happening, or if the climate is changing it is not because of human influence,[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-13)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-14) attempting to [sow doubt in the scientific consensus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate_change_denial "Climate change denial").[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-15)

Existence of a scientific consensus
-----------------------------------

[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/20220629_Public_estimates_of_scientific_consensus_on_climate_change_-_horizontal_bar_chart.svg/500px-20220629_Public_estimates_of_scientific_consensus_on_climate_change_-_horizontal_bar_chart.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:20220629_Public_estimates_of_scientific_consensus_on_climate_change_-_horizontal_bar_chart.svg)

 The public substantially underestimates the degree of scientific consensus that humans are causing climate change.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-16) Studies from 2019 to 2021[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-17)[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-18)[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-19) found scientific consensus to range from 98.7–100%.

Studies of the scientific opinion on climate change have been undertaken since the 1970s,[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-NDU1978-20) and they have been establishing widespread consensus since the 1990s,[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-Stewart1992-21)[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-BrayvonStorch1999-22) with the level of agreement increasing over time.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-23) Individual scientists, universities, and laboratories contribute to the scientific opinion on climate change via their [peer-reviewed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peer_review "Peer review")[publications](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_journal "Scientific journal"), while the scientific bodies of national or international standing summarise the areas of collective agreement and relative certainty in [synthesis reports](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Systematic_review "Systematic review").[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-Oreskes_consensus-24)

Examples of such reports include or the 2004 [Arctic Climate Impact Assessment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arctic_Climate_Impact_Assessment "Arctic Climate Impact Assessment") from the [International Arctic Science Committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Arctic_Science_Committee "International Arctic Science Committee") and the governments of the [Arctic Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arctic_Council "Arctic Council"),[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-amap-25)[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-Impacts_of_a_Warming_Arctic:_Arctic_Climate_Impact_Assessment_New_Scientific_Consensus:_Arctic_Is_Warming_Rapidly-26) or the United States' [National Climate Assessment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Climate_Assessment "National Climate Assessment"), which has been released periodically since 2000 under the auspices of the [United States Global Change Research Program](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Global_Change_Research_Program "United States Global Change Research Program"). The fourth NCA, released in 2017, involved the efforts of thirteen federal agencies, led by the [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") (NOAA),[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-USGCRP_participants-27) and around "1,000 people, including 300 leading scientists, roughly half from outside the government."[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-CNN_Christensen_2018-28)

The [Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intergovernmental_Panel_on_Climate_Change "Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change") (IPCC) had been formed by the [United Nations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly") in 1988,[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-29)[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-30) and it presents reports summarizing the strength and extent of consensus on climate change and [its numerous aspects](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change "Effects of climate change") to the [member states of the United Nations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations"), with the major reports released at 5-to-7-year intervals starting from 1990.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-31)

[![Image 7](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Ipcc_assessments_pages.png/250px-Ipcc_assessments_pages.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ipcc_assessments_pages.png)

Page counts of the six IPCC Assessment Reports (1990 to 2021)

 Between 1990 and 2023, the IPCC published six comprehensive assessment reports reviewing the latest climate science. The IPCC has also produced 14 special reports on particular topics.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-Intergovernmental_Panel_on_Climate_Change_UCS_IPCC_Who_are_they?-32) Each assessment report has four parts. These are a contribution from each of the three working groups, plus a synthesis report. The synthesis report integrates the working group contributions. It also integrates any special reports produced in that assessment cycle.

In 2001, science academies from 17 countries ([Australia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [Belgium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Brazil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), [Canada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China "China"), [France](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/France "France"), [Germany](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/India "India"), [Indonesia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Ireland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), [Italy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Malaysia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [New Zealand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [Sweden](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), [Trinidad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinidad "Trinidad"), [Turkey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and the [United Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")) made a joint statement endorsing the work of IPCC. They concurred that the temperatures are rising and will continue to rise due to human activities, and also stressed the importance of cutting [greenhouse gas emissions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions"), concluding that "Business as usual is no longer a viable option". It is also notable for being one of the first statements to explicitly use the term "consensus".[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-The_Science_of_Climate_Change-33) In 2005, another joint statement from the science academies of major countries (Brazil, Canada, China, France, Germany, India, Italy, Japan, Russia, United Kingdom and the United States referred to the conclusions of the IPCC as "the international scientific consensus", and urged prompt action on both [climate change mitigation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") and [climate change adaptation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate_change_adaptation "Climate change adaptation").[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-nationalacademies21-34) Elsewhere around the world, other organizations to have referred to the scientific consensus include [Network of African Science Academies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Network_of_African_Science_Academies "Network of African Science Academies") in 2007,[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-NASAC2007-35) and the [International Union for Quaternary Research](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Union_for_Quaternary_Research "International Union for Quaternary Research") in 2008.[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-inqua-36)

In 2013, a study which found that out of over 4,000 peer-reviewed papers on climate science published since 1990, 97% agree, explicitly or implicitly, that global warming is happening and is human-caused.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-37)[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-38) Surveys of scientists' views on climate change – with a focus on human caused climate change – have been undertaken since the 1970s.[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-NDU1978-20)[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-Stewart1992-21) A 2016 reanalysis confirmed that "the finding of 97% consensus [that humans are causing recent global warming] in published climate research is robust and consistent with other surveys of climate scientists and peer-reviewed studies."[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-39) A 2019 study found scientific consensus to be at 100%,[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-Powell2019-6) and a 2021 study found that consensus exceeded 99%.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change#cite_note-EnvRschLtrs_20211019-7)

More than 99.9% of studies agree: Humans caused climate change
==============================================================

By [Krishna Ramanujan](mailto:ksr32@cornell.edu "Email   Krishna Ramanujan
")
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

October 19, 2021[LinkedIn](https://news.cornell.edu/#linkedin)[Facebook](https://news.cornell.edu/#facebook)[Twitter](https://news.cornell.edu/#twitter)[Email](https://news.cornell.edu/#email)[Share](https://www.addtoany.com/share#url=https%3A%2F%2Fnews.cornell.edu%2Fstories%2F2021%2F10%2Fmore-999-studies-agree-humans-caused-climate-change&title=More%20than%2099.9%25%20of%20studies%20agree%3A%20Humans%20caused%20climate%20change)

More than 99.9% of peer-reviewed scientific papers agree that climate change is mainly caused by humans, according to a new survey of 88,125 climate-related studies.

The research updates a similar 2013 paper revealing that 97% of studies published between 1991 and 2012 supported the idea that human activities are altering Earth’s climate. The current survey examines the literature published from 2012 to November 2020 to explore whether the consensus has changed.

“We are virtually certain that the consensus is well over 99% now and that it’s pretty much case closed for any meaningful public conversation about the reality of human-caused climate change,” said Mark Lynas, a visiting fellow at the Alliance for Science and the paper’s first author.

Related Stories
---------------

[![Image 1](https://news.cornell.edu/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail_story_sm/public/0102_browning_0.jpg?itok=fVAiodvJ)](https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2024/01/most-adirondack-lakes-will-likely-become-unsuitable-trout "Most Adirondack lakes will likely become unsuitable for trout")

[Most Adirondack lakes will likely become unsuitable for trout](https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2024/01/most-adirondack-lakes-will-likely-become-unsuitable-trout)

[![Image 2](https://news.cornell.edu/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail_story_sm/public/0116_biochar_tnd_0.jpg?itok=8XWTaWH1)](https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2024/01/nys-first-dairy-farm-biochar-kiln-advances-green-agriculture "NY’s first dairy farm biochar kiln advances green agriculture")

[NY’s first dairy farm biochar kiln advances green agriculture](https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2024/01/nys-first-dairy-farm-biochar-kiln-advances-green-agriculture)

“It's critical to acknowledge the principal role of greenhouse gas emissions so that we can rapidly mobilize new solutions, since we are already witnessing in real time the devastating impacts of climate related disasters on businesses, people and the economy,” said Benjamin Houlton, the Ronald P. Lynch Dean of the College of Agriculture and Life Sciences and a co-author of the study, “[Greater than 99% Consensus on Human Caused Climate Change in the Peer-Reviewed Scientific Literature](https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.1088/1748-9326/ac2966),” which published Oct. 19 in the journal Environmental Research Letters.

In spite of such results, public opinion polls as well as opinions of politicians and public representatives point to false beliefs and claims that a significant debate still exists among scientists over the true cause of climate change. In 2016, the Pew Research Center found that only 27% of U.S. adults believe that “almost all” scientists agreed that climate change is due to human activity, according to the paper. A 2021 Gallup poll pointed to a deepening partisan divide in American politics on whether Earth’s rising observed temperatures since the Industrial Revolution were primarily caused by humans.

“To understand where a consensus exists, you have to be able to quantify it,” Lynas said. “That means surveying the literature in a coherent and non-arbitrary way in order to avoid trading cherry-picked papers, which is often how these arguments are carried out in the public sphere.”

In the study, the researchers began by examining a random sample of 3,000 studies from the dataset of 88,125 English-language climate papers published between 2012 and 2020. They found only found four out of the 3,000 papers were skeptical of human-caused climate change. “We knew that [climate skeptical papers] were vanishingly small in terms of their occurrence, but we thought there still must be more in the 88,000,” Lynas said.

Co-author Simon Perry, a United Kingdom-based software engineer and volunteer at the Alliance for Science, created an algorithm that searched out keywords from papers the team knew were skeptical, such as “solar,” “cosmic rays” and “natural cycles.” The algorithm was applied to all 88,000-plus papers, and the program ordered them so the skeptical ones came higher in the order. They found many of these dissenting papers near the top, as expected, with diminishing returns further down the list. Overall, the search yielded 28 papers that were implicitly or explicitly skeptical, all published in minor journals.

If the 97% result from the 2013 study still left some doubt on scientific consensus on the human influence on climate, the current findings go even further to allay any uncertainty, Lynas said. “This pretty much should be the last word,” he said.

Scientific Consensus
====================

[![Image 39](https://science.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/gavin-2024-combined-temperature-observations.jpg?w=1614)](https://science.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/gavin-2024-combined-temperature-observations.jpg)

Temperature data showing rapid warming in the past few decades, the latest data going up through 2023. According to NASA, Earth’s average surface temperature in 2023 was the warmest on record since recordkeeping began in 1880, continuing a long-term trend of rising global temperatures. On top of that, the 10 most recent years have been the hottest.

NASA's Goddard Institute for Space Studies

It’s important to remember that scientists always focus on the evidence, not on opinions. Scientific [evidence](https://science.nasa.gov/climate/evidence) continues to show that human activities ([primarily the human burning of fossil fuels](https://science.nasa.gov/climate/causes)) have warmed Earth’s surface and its ocean basins, which in turn have [continued to impact Earth’s climate](https://science.nasa.gov/climate/effects). This is based on over a century of scientific evidence forming the structural backbone of today's civilization.

NASA Global Climate Change presents the state of scientific knowledge about climate change while highlighting the role NASA plays in better understanding our home planet. This effort includes citing multiple peer-reviewed studies from research groups across the world,[](https://climate.nasa.gov/scientific-consensus/#footnote_1)[1](https://science.nasa.gov/climate-change/scientific-consensus/#footnote_1) illustrating the accuracy and consensus of research results (in this case, the scientific consensus on climate change) consistent with NASA’s scientific research portfolio.

With that said, multiple studies published in peer-reviewed scientific journals[](https://climate.nasa.gov/scientific-consensus/#footnote_1)[1](https://science.nasa.gov/climate-change/scientific-consensus/#footnote_1) show that climate-warming trends over the past century are extremely likely due to human activities. In addition, most of the leading scientific organizations worldwide have issued public statements endorsing this position. The following is a partial list of these organizations, along with links to their published statements and a selection of related resources.

Abstract
--------

Communicating the scientific consensus that human-caused climate change is real increases climate change beliefs, worry and support for public action in the United States. In this preregistered experiment, we tested two scientific consensus messages, a classic message on the reality of human-caused climate change and an updated message additionally emphasizing scientific agreement that climate change is a crisis. Across online convenience samples from 27 countries (_n_ = 10,527), the classic message substantially reduces misperceptions (_d_ = 0.47, 95% CI (0.41, 0.52)) and slightly increases climate change beliefs (from _d_ = 0.06, 95% CI (0.01, 0.11) to _d_ = 0.10, 95% CI (0.04, 0.15)) and worry (_d_ = 0.05, 95% CI (−0.01, 0.10)) but not support for public action directly. The updated message is equally effective but provides no added value. Both messages are more effective for audiences with lower message familiarity and higher misperceptions, including those with lower trust in climate scientists and right-leaning ideologies. Overall, scientific consensus messaging is an effective, non-polarizing tool for changing misperceptions, beliefs and worry across different audiences.

# Do scientists agree on climate change?

Yes, the vast majority of actively publishing climate scientists – 97 percent – agree that humans are causing global warming and climate change. Most of the leading science organizations around the world have issued public statements expressing this, including international and U.S. science academies, the United Nations Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, and a whole host of reputable scientific bodies around the world. A list of these organizations is provided [here](https://climate.nasa.gov/scientific-consensus/).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent studies show a consensus exceeding 99% among climate scientists that human activities are the primary driver of climate change, reflecting an unprecedented agreement in the field.
Ongoing research is crucial to further understand climate change mechanisms and impacts, enabling effective adaptation and mitigation strategies to combat its adverse effects.
Communicating scientific consensus on climate change is vital to shift public perceptions, increase awareness, and galvanize support for policies aimed at addressing this global crisis.
